1As an MSK-type CPM, ARTM1/SOQPSK-TG can be interpreted (with approximation) as an OQPSK waveform. Under this interpretation, FEC codes designed for binary phase shift keying (BPSK) can be applied to SOQPSKTG [3]. This OQPSK-based approach is currently taken in IRIG-106 as the sole option for FEC in telemetry. However, a more general CPM-based FEC approach for SOQPSK-TG has never been explored.
2In fact, at the 08-Aug-2024 meeting of the RF Systems Group (RFSC) of the Range Commander’s Council (RCC) (the standards body that maintains IRIG-106), the LDPC codes presented herein for ARTM0 and ARTM2 were provisionally approved for adopted into the IRIG-106 standard.

(SOQPSK-TG). This has approximately twice the spectrum

The desired low density parity check (LDPC) continuous phase modulation (CPM) coding scheme is shown in Fig. 1. It consists of an LDPC encoder and a CPM modulator that are separated by an interleaver (denoted by the symbol Π). In this paper, we are interested in the three CPM schemes that are widely used in aeronautical telemetry. The original of these is a legacy waveform that dates back to the 1960s and is commonly known as pulse code modulation/frequency modulation (PCM/FM). Due to the spectrum inefficiency of PCM/FM, the Advanced Range TeleMetry program (ARTM) developed two “tiers” of spectrum-efficient CPM waveforms, which were adopted into the IRIG-106 telemetry standard [1] in the early 2000s. The first of these is known as the telemetry group version of shaped offset quadrature phase shift keying

None of these waveforms has a modern, capacityapproaching FEC solution available.1 As such, the LDPC– CPM codes we present herein have the practical motivation of having immediate application within the IRIG-106 standard and would enable a degree of power efficiency/range extension and data quality in aeronautical telemetry that is unheard of today.2
Our approach to designing LDPC codes for the CPM-based scheme in Fig. 1 draws primarily from references [4]–[8] with modification and adaptation as needed. A critical step is that the design philosophy must begin with a characterization of the phenomenon known as extrinsic information transfer (EXIT) [9] that pertains to the CPM scheme itself. The EXIT properties of the CPM waveform are thus central to the for-

Fig. 2. Power spectral densities (PSDs) of the three CPM schemes of interest in this paper. These are expressed in dB relative to an unmodulated carrier (dBc) and frequency normalized to the coded bit rate, Rc = 1/Tc, i.e. the bit rate presented at the input to the CPM modulator.

mation of the LDPC code that is matched to it. We focus only on quasi-cyclic LDPC codes, primarily because the existing IRIG-106/AR4JA codes [7] are of this type, but also because they enjoy the twin advantages of good performance and low encoding complexity. We use the protomatrix EXIT (PEXIT) design technique [4] with modifications as necessary to incorporate the CPM EXIT characteristics, similar to [5]. The PEXIT algorithm is particularly attractive due to its simplicity and accuracy in predicting the final performance of the code. Furthermore, a number of additional design considerations (such as error floor performance) can be incorporated into the PEXIT design process.
The remainder of the paper is organized as follows. In Section II we cover the preliminary topics needed for the LDPC code design procedure. In Section III we develop the LDPC–CPM code design procedure itself and discuss the results of the protomatrix decoding threshold optimization step. Finally, in Section IV we present bit error rate (BER) results and offer concluding remarks.

II. PRELIMINARIES ON LDPC CODE DESIGN

A. Protomatrix Basics

A very large quasi-cyclic LDPC code can be specified by a relatively small protomatrix (or protograph), which is then “lifted” to form the much larger, final version of the parity check matrix for the code. A protomatrix has the following form

 b0,0

 b1,0

B=  

...

b0,1

···

b0,NB−1 

b1,1 ...

   

(1)

bMB−1,0 bMB−1,1 · · · bMB−1,NB−1

which has dimensions MB×NB. The columns of B correspond to “variable nodes” and we index the columns with 0 ≤ j ≤

NB − 1. The rows of B correspond to “check nodes” and we index the rows with 0 ≤ i ≤ MB − 1. The elements of B are drawn from a B-ary alphabet, i.e. bi,j ∈ {0, 1, . . . , B − 1}. The sum, or “weight,” of the j-th column is referred to as the variable node degree, dv(j); likewise, dc(i) is the degree of the i-th row, or check node degree.

Authorized licensed use limited to: Yonsei Univ. Downloaded on June 24,2026 at 16:13:40 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.

MILCOM 2024 Track 1 - Waveforms and Signal Processing

are given in the Appendix of [9]. This function provides a simple translation between the σ domain and the I (information) domain, and its inverse provides the reverse translation [these functions are as simple to use as, for example, cos(·) and cos−1(·)].
The PEXIT analysis [4] (and the original EXIT analysis [9] before that) employs a structure that mimics the LDPC decoder itself. However, instead of passing actual received channel samples to the actual LDPC decoder and its very large parity check matrix, the PEXIT approach operates on the much smaller protomatrix and models the flow of information within this matrix. It begins by injecting an initial amount of information into the protomatrix (a simple number in the range 0 ≤ I ≤ 1), this translates into an initial value of σ [by a simple call to the function J−1(I)]. From there, the values of σ flow from variable nodes to check nodes in the protomatrix in an iterative fashion, accumulating in the process by repeated use of simple arithmetic such as in (4). The initial amount of information is related to the SNR, again through the function J(σ), and the desired result is that σ accumulates iteratively without bound, which translates to I → 1, i.e. convergence to an error free result. The PEXIT computations are surprisingly simple and remarkably accurate. The structure of the LDPC code can be adjusted, “tweaked,” or otherwise optimized until the lowest SNR is found that still results in the information converging to unity. This is the decoding threshold SNR.
A major incompatability with the PEXIT analysis and our current application is that the CPM modulator/demodulator is situated between the LDPC encoder/decoder and the AWGN channel. This fundamentally alters the flow of extrinsic information in the system. This issue was addressed in studies such as [5], [6] and requires only that we evaluate the EXIT “characteristic function” of the modulation scheme, which we explain next.

of J( 8Es/N0) equal to 1/2, 2/3, and 4/5. Because J(·) represents the capacity of the binary-input AWGN channel, these values of Es/N0 correspond to the maximum achievable code rates of 1/2, 2/3, and 4/5. That these curves are horizontal lines underscores the fact that, for BPSK, the extrinsic a posteriori information is independent of the a priori information.
In the case of CPM, it is not possible to compute these quantities in closed form and so we resort to numerical evaluation via Monte Carlo simulation. The procedure is depicted in block-diagram form in Fig. 3. From an outside perspective, the independent (input) variable is IA, the a priori information, which we increment in steps of 0.01 (1/100) through the range 0.0 ≤ IA ≤ 1.0. For each value of IA, we compute (by simulation) a corresponding dependent (output) value of IE, which is the extrinsic a posteriori information. The simulation is parameterized by the channel SNR, Es/N0, which remains fixed during a full “sweep” of the input IA values. This results in an input–output “transfer characteristic,” IE = TEs/N0 [IA], for each SNR value. This is simply a LUT that returns a value of IE for a given value of IA at the specified SNR. We use square brackets [·] to denote that the LUT index must be rounded to the nearest 1/100 in order to access the nearest output. We compute these LUTs (curves) for as many values of SNR as are needed for the PEXIT algorithm in [4], [5], in Es/N0 increments of 0.05 dB.
From a perspective internal to Fig. 3, the CPM softinput soft-output (SISO) module [11] is at the center of this simulation. The SISO module has two inputs, and we obtain these inputs using a few random number generators:

• A long sequence of equiprobable random “information”

bits, u, are generated and applied in several places in

the simulation. They are first passed along the “signal

path” where filtering (the

they undergo modulation, AWGN, MF bank), and scaling by the factor

m√aEtcshLecd,

thus forming the upper (“code”) input to the SISO

module. They are also passed along the path that forms

the lower (a priori L-value) input to the SISO module,

where they are converted to the binary antipodal alphabet

and properly scaled so they form the “mean” component

of the a priori L-values, like so: σA2 /2 · a(u), where σA = J−1(IA) and a(u) = 2u − 1 is a simple mapping

from the logical alphabet to the antipodal alphabet. And

finally, the “information” bits are applied at the very end

of the simulation to compute the conditional mean of the

SISO’s extrinsic output.

• The “noise” component of the a priori L-values is ob-

tained from a Gaussian random number generator with zero mean and variance σA2 , where σA = J −1(IA). • The AWGN that is randomly generated for the signal path is parameterized by Es/N0. The SISO module outputs a sequence of extrinsic a posteriori L-values, {λi(u; O)}, belonging to the input sequence u (i is a sequence index). As was developed above, if we condition this output with the input sequence [i.e. multiply the output by a(ui)], then the result can be modeled as a Gaussian random variable with variance σE2 and mean σE2/2. The overall output of the simulation is thus

IE = J 2 · a(ui)λi(u; O)

(8)

where (·) denotes the sample mean (average) of a sequence.3 While the results of these simulations are available only at
discrete values of IA, for conceptual purposes we can treat TEs/N0 (IA) as being a continuous function of IA. As was pointed out in [6], a commonly-accepted generalization of the results in [12] is that the mean of the curve TEs/N0 (IA) serves as an upper bound on the the maximum achievable code rate for the given value of Es/N0:

and (d), respectively, for the values of Es/N0 that correspond to maximum achievable code rates of 1/2, 2/3, and 4/5. We

call particular attention to Fig. 4 (c), which shows the EXIT

curves for ARTM1. Because ARTM1 (SOQPSK-TG) is an

MSK-type CPM, it is possible to (1) turn off the differential encoder (DE), and (2) approximate the signal as OQPSK,

which in turn is identical to BPSK from a channel capacity

perspective [13]. Although the ARTM1 curves in Fig. 4 (c)

were computed numerically, the curves for DE off are identical to the BPSK curves in Fig. 4 (a) (aside from minor differences

in the Es/N0 values needed to achieve the desired code rates). This confirms the findings presented in [14] for MSK, which

is that the EXIT behavior is markedly different depending

on the status of DE (on or off), and that the EXIT behavior with DE off is identical to that of BPSK. This EXIT behavior supports the conclusion in [14] that codes that are optimal for

BPSK are likewise optimal with “nonrecursive MSK” (as it

is called in [14]), which is akin here to ARTM1 with DE off. Thus, Fig. 4 (c) demonstrates exactly why the BPSK-optimized

AR4JA codes [7] perform well when paired with ARTM1 with

DE off, as currently embodied in the IRIG-106 standard. The three plots in Fig. 4 (b)–(d) also demonstrate the need for the

present study, because the optimization of the AR4JA codes

for BPSK (i.e. for EXIT curves that are horizontal) does not translate to ARTM0, ARTM2, and ARTM1 with DE on.
A final observation regarding Fig. 4 is that the EXIT transfer characteristics have a positive slope and join the point (1, 1) (except for ARTM1 with DE off, as discussed earlier). This means that an increase in a priori information supplied to the CPM SISO module (i.e. increasing abscissa) results in a gain in extrinsic a posteriori information produced by the CPM SISO module (i.e. increasing ordinate). This provides an information theoretic justification for an iterative decoder that alternates “globally” between a CPM SISO and an LDPC decoder (the LDPC decoder may still “self iterate” with local iterations, but global iterations that include the CPM SISO will result in information gains). Conversely, the flat EXIT curves for BPSK (or ARTM1 with DE off) mean that additional passes through the demodulator (global iterations) are pointless because no gains in extrinsic information are possible.
D. CPM-Based PEXIT Algorithm The modifications to the PEXIT algorithm that are necessary
for CPM are essentially the same as those that were developed in [5] for partial response channels. The original algorithm in [4] maintains a priori (“A”) and extrinsic (“E”) variables going to/from the variable (“v,” indexed by j) and check (“c,” indexed by i) nodes that are updated iteratively, with input coming externally from the “channel.” In this case, the “channel” is the CPM SISO module (“s”), which itself has an iterative update from a priori to extrinsic that involves

Authorized licensed use limited to: Yonsei Univ. Downloaded on June 24,2026 at 16:13:40 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.

MILCOM 2024 Track 1 - Waveforms and Signal Processing

the EXIT characteristic function for the SNR of the AWGN channel. The primary points where the algorithm needs to be modified are where the “channel” interfaces with the LDPC decoder, because in the present context the “channel” is the CPM SISO module.
In the initialization step, the user specifies Es/N0, which in turn selects a particular LUT (curve) of the CPM EXIT characteristic function TEs/N0 [·]. We set IAs = 0, which is the a priori information for the SISO module and we enter the PEXIT iterative loop. In the SISO update step, we quantize whatever the current value of IAs is to the nearest available input index in the LUT, TEs/N0 [·], and then access the LUT to convert the a priori information to extrinsic information: IEs = TEs/N0 [IAs].
To transfer information to the LDPC decoder, we set Ich(j) = IEs, ∀j, except we set Ich(j) = 0 if the j-th variable node is punctured. This is the a priori information going from the “channel” to the j-th variable node of the LDPC decoder. From there, the PEXIT iteration proceeds exactly as outlined in [4]. At the end of the PEXIT iteration, when it is time to transfer information back to the SISO module, the update is given by

(10)

It is worth pointing out that setting Ich(j) = IEs, ∀j implies that the SISO module transfers a uniform amount of information to the variable nodes, which does not vary from node to node (i.e. it is independent of j). Likewise, even though Equation (10) operates on information values from variable nodes that varies with j, they are averaged over j so that a uniform amount of information is transferred back to the SISO module. This assumption of uniformity back and forth between the SISO module and the LDPC decoder in the PEXIT algorithm is akin to the functionality [15] of the interleaver and deinterleaver in the receiver.

III. LDPC CODE DESIGN FOR CPM

A. Protomatrix Optimization In this section, we propose a simple procedure to search
for a good protomatrix (protograph) that is “matched” with a specific CPM scheme. The desired properties of the resulting protomatrix are a low decoding threshold and preservation of the linear minimum distance growth property [7], which guarantees no error floor when assigning random circulants in the lifting stage of the design.
To preserve the linear minimum distance growth property, there are several techniques and strategies that can be employed; such as [7]: (a) including some degree-1 variable nodes; (b) including some degree-2 variable nodes via the check-node splitting technique; (c) including some punctured variable nodes; (d) including some high-degree variable nodes; and (e) maintaining a variable node degree of 3 or higher except for the controlled introduction of the degree-1 and degree-2 nodes mentioned earlier. All of the above techniques

were explored in searching for LDPC codes to pair with ARTM0, ARTM1, and ARTM2. We did not observe any advantage in by employing degree-1 and degree-2 variable nodes, or by introducing punctured variable nodes. Our best decoding threshold results were obtained by a simple search that focused on variable nodes with a degree of 3 or more, i.e. dv(j) ≥ 3, ∀j.
A basic question is to determine the relative size of the protomatrix. We found that if there are too few rows, then there does not seem to be sufficient degrees of freedom to achieve a low decoding threshold. For example, the AR4JA codes have a protomatrix with only three rows [7]. While this achieves a low decoding threshold for BPSK, the shape of the EXIT characteristic curves are fundamentally different for BPSK vs. CPM: horizontal vs. varying from low to high; and a demodulator not in the decoding loop vs. a demodulator in the decoding loop. On the other hand, our observations were that too many rows in the protomatrix has negative implications in terms of slower decoding convergence (i.e. a larger number of required decoding iterations) and difficulty in assigning random circulants in the lifting stage of the design.
Based on these investigations, we settled on a basic protomatrix format with MB = 4 rows and NB columns as required to achieve the desired code rate:

b0,0 b0,1 · · · b0,NB−1

(11)

This is consistent with the studies in [5], [6]. We allow the elements of B to be drawn from a 4-ary alphabet, i.e. bi,j ∈ {0, 1, 2, 3}, with B = 4. The remaining task, while of the utmost importance, is simply to specify the elements of B.
To accomplish this, we optimize the elements of B on a column-wise basis (i.e. on a variable node by variable node basis), which is similar to the approach in [5], [6]. With a 4ary alphabet and four elements per column, there is an alphabet of 44 = 256 possible columns, with a column sum (column weight, or variable node degree) ranging from 0 to 12. We eliminate any columns with a weight less than three and we refer to this initial set of candidate columns as Sinit. Next, we commence the CPM-based PEXIT algorithm, which was described in Section II-D. The algorithm must be supplied with a set of EXIT characteristic function curves (LUTs) for the specific CPM scheme that are computed (simulated) over a sufficiently large range of Es/N0 values. Fig. 4 gives only three such curves for each of the CPMs of interest in this study, but a much larger set of curves was computed in order to support the code design procedure.
A starting value of Es/N0, (Es/N0)cur, is selected and a full execution of the algorithm takes place for each of the candidate columns in Sprev = Sinit (the remainder of the protomatrix remains constant while the candidate column changes from one PEXIT execution to the next). Any candidate columns that achieve the desired result (IAPP(j) = 1, ∀j) are deemed “survivors” and are collected in the set Scur. After

Authorized licensed use limited to: Yonsei Univ. Downloaded on June 24,2026 at 16:13:40 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.

MILCOM 2024 Track 1 - Waveforms and Signal Processing

each candidate in Sprev is evaluated, the results of the current step are saved as (Es/N0)prev = (Es/N0)cur and Sprev = Scur, after which the value of (Es/N0)cur is decremented (by 0.05 dB in our case). The PEXIT algorithm is again executed once for each of the survivors in Sprev, which produces a new list of survivors, Scur, that belong to (Es/N0)cur. This continues until (Es/N0)cur is decremented to the point where Scur is empty (i.e. no survivors), whereupon (Es/N0)prev and Sprev represent the end result of the optimization for the given column. An “optimized” value for the column can be selected from Sprev and fixed into the protomatrix. The focus then shifts to another column and can begin at (Es/N0)prev using Sprev = Sinit. At some point, each column has been visited enough that no further progress is made and the decoding threshold is determined to be (Es/N0)∗ = (Es/N0)prev.
It is quite apparent that this approach does not lead to a unique optimization result; however, there is some reassuring consistency in the results. The entire protomatrix must exist in some (unoptimized) form prior to the attempt to optimize the first column. In repeated optimization runs, we have not observed any particular sensitivity to the initial form of the protomatrix. Rather, we have found that if the initial optimization starts “somewhere,” and each column, in turn, is visited by the optimization a couple of times, the same final decoding threshold value (Es/N0)∗ is achieved with a similar distribution of variable and check node degrees. This is consistent with other studies on LDPC code design, cf. e.g. [5]–[7], [9].
B. Two-Stage Lifting The analysis in [16], [17] showed certain performance
advantages are possessed by the two-stage lifting procedure that was used to obtain the AR4JA codes [7] and similar codes, e.g. [5]. This provides clear motivation for us to follow a similar approach. We are interested in generating final LDPC codes with dimensions similar to the AR4JA codes that have been adopted in the IRIG 106 standard [1], i.e. the first three columns of Table I. However, slightly different from the AR4JA codes, we use a lifting factor of 2 in the first stage (a factor of 4 was used in the first stage for AR4JA). This smaller factor in the first stage allows us to use a larger factor in the second and final stage.

Recall that the elements of B are drawn from a 4-ary alphabet, i.e. bi,j ∈ {0, 1, 2, 3}. When we lift the 4-ary elements bi,j by a factor of 2, each element bi,j is replaced by a 2 × 2 sub-matrix, as follows:

0→

0 0

0 0

,

1→

1 0

0 1

,

2→

1 1

1 1

,

3→

21 12

This results in an “intermediate” protomatrix, D, with dimensions MD = 2MB and ND = 2NB. Given the 4-ary alphabet and the lifting factor of 2, it is not possible to construct these 2 × 2 sub-matrixes with elements drawn only from the binary alphabet {0, 1}. In other words, when expressed as protographs, the original protograph was allowed to have multiple parallel edges (protomatrix elements greater than 1), and likewise for the intermediate protograph; however, after the final lifting stage, all multiple parallel edges will be eliminated and the matrix elements are drawn only from the binary alphabet {0, 1}.
In the second and final lifting stage, D is lifted by a factor of ML by assigning random phases to ML × ML circulants using the ACE algorithm [8]. A circulant is a square matrix where each row is a right-hand circular (barrel) shift of the row above. As such, the entire circulant can be specified by providing only the first row; the “weight” of the circulant is the sum of the first row; the location of a non-zero element of the first row is its “phase.” Because of our first lifting stage, the intermediate protomatrix contains entries drawn only from the alphabet {0, 1, 2}, and so this second lifting stage results in weight-2, weight-1, or weight-0 (all-zeros) circulants. A weight-1 circulant can be specified simply by its phase, which is an integer in the set {0, 1, . . . , ML − 1}. We simplify the discussion by considering a weight-2 circulant to be the superposition of two weight-1 circulants with different phases.
This second lifting stage results in the final low-density parity check matrix, H, with dimensions M = MLMD and N = MLND. The companion to this is the generator matrix, G, with dimensions K = N − M and N .

With additional trial and error, for K = 1024 we selected the protograph B0b,2/3 which has a slightly worse decoding threshold of (Es/N0)∗ = −1.25 dB but resulted in excellent BER performance.
ARTM1. Applying (9) on the EXIT characteristic functions belonging to ARTM1, the SNR values that correspond to the maximum achievable rates of interest are (Es/N0)achievable ∈ {−2.85, −0.60, +1.25} dB. We were able to design protomatrixes for these code rates with decoding thresholds (Es/N0)∗ ∈ {−1.90, 0.0, +1.80} dB, which is a gap to capacity of {0.95, 0.60, 0.55} dB (again, less than on dB). The resulting protomatrixes are shown in Eq. (13). As with the ARTM0 case, we settled on two different protomatrixes for the rate-2/3 case for ARTM1, in order to address the shallower BER slope observed for the K = 1024 block length. The decoding threshold for B1b,2/3 is slightly degraded at (Es/N0)∗ = +0.10 dB but resulted in excellent BER performance.
By way of comparison, the protomatrixes for the AR4JA codes have decoding thresholds of (Es/N0)∗ ∈ {−2.40, −0.25, +1.60} dB when applied to ARTM1 with DE off, which is a smaller gap to capacity of {0.45, 0.35, 0.35} dB. It has already been established in [14] that LDPC codes

ARTM2. Applying (9) on the EXIT characteristic functions belonging to ARTM2, the SNR values that correspond to the maximum achievable rates of interest are (Es/N0)achievable ∈ {+0.40, +2.75, +4.60} dB. We were able to design protomatrixes for these code rates with decoding thresholds (Es/N0)∗ ∈ {+1.75, +3.60, +5.50} dB, which is a gap to capacity of {+1.35, +0.85, +0.90} dB (nearly one dB). No shallowing of the BER slope was observed when lifting these protomatrixes to the various block lengths. The resulting protomatrixes are shown in Eq. (14).
IV. BER PERFORMANCE AND CONCLUSION Figs. 5, 6, and 7 show bit error rate (BER) and frame error rate (FER) simulations for the 6 LDPC codes belonging to, respectively, ARTM0, ARTM1, and ARTM2. Table I lists their coding gains, which range from 7.1 to 10.9 dB. The coding gains are denoted as ∆0, ∆1, and ∆2 [in dB] for ARTM0, ARTM1, and ARTM2, respectively, and use the uncoded BER = 10−8 crossing points of Eb/N0 ∈ {10.8, 12.9, 13.3} dB as a reference. The gains are comparable across the code and modulation types, which validates the consistency of the PEXIT-based design approach.

For ARTM0 and ARTM2, it is worth emphasizing that our basis of comparison is the uncoded case because no FEC option currently exists in the IRIG-106 standard for these modulations. For ARTM1, we have already noted that the existing AR4JA option in the IRIG-106 standard is superior by around 0.5 dB in terms of decoding threshold, and we note this advantage persists in the BER context. Thus, for the ARTM1 case, we do not put our LDPC–CPM codes forward as candidates for adoption in the IRIG-106 standard.
Overall, these LDPC codes paired with ARTM0 (PCM/FM) and ARTM 2 (ARTM CPM) can be considered to fill in the “missing” LDPC coding options in the current version of IRIG-106. Furthermore, because these three waveforms essentially span the entire parameter space for CPM, the LDPC–CPM code design procedure presented herein can be considered to be general and is applicable to all CPMs.
